segments.title: The dreadnought race was three contests at once
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke separates the dreadnought era into an Anglo-German numbers contest, an international design competition, and a South American naval race.
segments.body: Popular histories often reduce the dreadnought race to Britain and Germany, a framing Dr. Clarke argues is both Eurocentric and incomplete. Their rivalry was a quantitative contest shaped by Germany's risk-fleet theory: build enough ships that Britain would hesitate to fight for fear of losing its wider position. He describes that strategic logic as convoluted and ineffective in practice. At the same time, Britain, Italy, Japan, and the United States competed qualitatively by producing increasingly capable designs, including super-dreadnoughts and the Queen Elizabeth class. Argentina, Brazil, and Chile conducted a third dreadnought competition in South America. The passage therefore gives viewers a compact way to distinguish fleet numbers, warship quality, and regional rivalry instead of treating the era as one Anglo-German contest.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes the Anglo-German quantitative race and explains Germany's risk-fleet logic.
The transcript identifies a qualitative design race among Britain, Italy, Japan, and America, plus a South American race among Argentina, Brazil, and Chile.
